subject: Physics
title: Cavendish Weighs the Earth
description: A micro-lesson that makes Cavendish Weighs the Earth usable.

hook.text: In 1798, Henry Cavendish measured gravity between small lead balls—and used it to calculate Earth's mass.

buildup.text: He suspended two small lead balls on a wire near two large ones. The gravitational pull twisted the wire.

discovery.text: By measuring the tiny twist, Cavendish determined the gravitational constant G—Newton's missing number.

twist.text: With G known, he could calculate Earth's mass: about 6 × 10^24 kilograms. He literally 'weighed the world.'

climax.text: His experiment was so sensitive that someone walking nearby would ruin it. He observed by telescope from outside.

punchline.text: Lead balls and a twisted wire. That's how you weigh a planet.

quiz.question: What did Cavendish's experiment ultimately determine?
